coredata.dc:title: All-in-one magnetic beads: Searching, capturing and simultaneous detection of dual tumor related proteins in real samples
coredata.dc:creator: Zou, Mingjing
Gong, Yan
Sun, Xinxin
Ding, Caifeng
coredata.dc:description: Simultaneous assay of multiple tumor marker proteins is of great importance for accurate diagnosis of cancer because most of cancers are usually related to various tumor-related proteins. In this work, we prepared yellow-colored carbon dots (y-CDs) and blue-colored carbon dots (b-CDs) for simultaneous detection of carcinoembryonic antigen (CEA) and cancer antigen125 (CA125) based on the antifouling magnetic beads (MBs) in real samples. Two sandwich structures were constructed by MBs modified with aptamer 1(Apt1) of proteins and aptamer 2(Apt2) tagged with CDs in the presence of target proteins. After CDs were cut off and separated, the fluorescence intensities at 430 nm (b-CDs) and 550 nm (y-CDs), relating to quantities of two proteins, were measured at a single excitation wavelength, obtaining the detection limits of 0.11 ng mL−1 (CEA) and 0.57 U mL−1 (CA125) respectively. Due to the target specific searching, recognition, capturing with aptamers, magnetic separation and antifouling property of peptides, the simultaneous differentiating and assaying CEA and CA125 in human serum can be easily operated, and results reveal a good correlation with data from the electrochemiluminescent (ECL) immunoassay in hospitals, demonstrating the promising candidate for clinical diagnosis of ovarian cancer.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carcionembryonic antigen
Cancer antigen 125
Magnetic beads
Dual-colored carbon dots
Antifouling

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of CEA-Apt2/b-CD and CA125-Apt2/y-CD probes
Simultaneous detection of CEA and CA125
Simultaneous detection of CEA and CA125 with antifouling peptide
Assay of samples in human serum

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM image and size distribution of b-CDs (a) and y-CDs(b), HRTEM image of b-CDs (c) and y-CDs(d), XPS of b-CDs(e) and y-CDs (f).
Fluorescence spectra of b-CDs (a), y-CDs(b) and b-CDs/y-CDs (c).
Contact angle images of MBs (a) and MBs/peptides (b), fluorescence intensity of FITC-BSA in MBs (c) and MBs/peptides supernatant (d).

(a) Fluorescence intensity at 430 and 550 nm enhancing respectively with concentration of CEA (0, 0.5, 1, 2, 4, 6, 8, 10, 12, 14, 18, 22, 26 ng mL−1), CA125 (0, 2, 5, 10, 20, 30, 40, 50, 75, 100, 125, 150, 175 U mL−1) increasing on MBs/peptides/CDs platform. (b) Linear relationship between ΔF
430 nm and concentration of CEA, ΔF
430 nm = 213.25c
CEA + 43.81(r = 0.9970). (c) Linear relationship between ΔF
550 nm and concentration of CA125, ΔF
550 nm = 21.23 c CA125 + 0.55(r = 0.9964). (d)Specificity of assays on MBs/CDs and MBs/peptides/CDs platforms. (e-f) Linear regression analysis of simultaneous detection and ECL immunoassay for CEA and CA125.
Process of simultaneous detection of CEA and CA125 based on MBs-assisted antifouling, recognition and separation.
Simultaneous determination of CEA and CA125 in normal serum.

head.ce:title: All-in-one magnetic beads: Searching, capturing and simultaneous detection of dual tumor related proteins in real samples

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Molecular Engineering, Qingdao University of Science and Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Simultaneous assay of multiple tumor marker proteins is of great importance for accurate diagnosis of cancer because most of cancers are usually related to various tumor-related proteins. In this work, we prepared yellow-colored carbon dots (y-CDs) and blue-colored carbon dots (b-CDs) for simultaneous detection of carcinoembryonic antigen (CEA) and cancer antigen125 (CA125) based on the antifouling magnetic beads (MBs) in real samples. Two sandwich structures were constructed by MBs modified with aptamer 1(Apt1) of proteins and aptamer 2(Apt2) tagged with CDs in the presence of target proteins. After CDs were cut off and separated, the fluorescence intensities at 430 nm (b-CDs) and 550 nm (y-CDs), relating to quantities of two proteins, were measured at a single excitation wavelength, obtaining the detection limits of 0.11 ng mL−1 (CEA) and 0.57 U mL−1 (CA125) respectively. Due to the target specific searching, recognition, capturing with aptamers, magnetic separation and antifouling property of peptides, the simultaneous differentiating and assaying CEA and CA125 in human serum can be easily operated, and results reveal a good correlation with data from the electrochemiluminescent (ECL) immunoassay in hospitals, demonstrating the promising candidate for clinical diagnosis of ovarian cancer.

In this work, yellow-colored carbon dots (y-CDs) and bule-colored dots (b-CDs) were prepared for the simultaneous detection of carcinoembryonic antigen (CEA) and cancer antigen125 (CA125) based on the antifouling magnetic beads (MBs) in real samples. The detection result has a good correlation with data from the electrochemiluminescent (ECL) immunoassay used in hospitals, demonstrating the promising candidate for clinical diagnosis of ovarian cancer.
ga1

ce:section.ce:para: Ovarian cancer has the highest mortality in all gynecological malignancies due to the lack of early warning symptoms. The five-year survival rate remains less than 30 % for majority of patients, who are currently diagnosed with terminal ovarian cancer patients, while the five-year survival rate of 95 % can be realized when the patients are diagnosed at the early state [1,2]. In addition, the detection of single tumor marker sometimes shows the inaccurate diagnosis because one biomarker can indicate a variety of disease. For example, carcinoembryonic antigen (CEA), which is one of the most extensively used cell-surface cancer markers, can indicate a variety of malignant and epithelial tumors cancers including ovary, liver, lung, breast, pancreas, and prostate cancers [3]. Thus, to reduce the mortality and morbidity of ovarian cancer, meanwhile, to improve the accuracy and efficiency, the simultaneous detection of multiple markers, such as CEA and cancer antigen 125 (CA125), is significantly important for its early diagnosis [4,5]. Furthermore, results by various research groups also prove that a dual/multiple-marker combining assay is more suitable than the single one for the serological screening with the advantage of accuracy, low cost, rapid assay and small amount of sample [6–8].

Immunological methods have been widely used for CEA and CA125 detection, such as colorimetric, electrochemical, fluorometric, microfluidic and electrochemiluminescent (ECL) immunoassay [9–13]. Among them, ECL immunoassay with high sensitivity is commonly used in clinical lab, which requires expensive instrument and reagent. Due to the advantages of convenience, high sensitivity and low cost, fluorescent methods for multiple tumor-related biomarker assay have been developed [14,15]. However, the emissive fluorescence signals were usually collected by various excitation wavelengths, leading to the obvious background noises [16]. Hence, simultaneous fluorescent analysis of multiple targets under the single excited state is incredibly valuable.

Aptamers are single-stranded DNA or RNA oligonucleotides by exponential enrichment (SELEX) process, which can recognize small molecules, proteins and cells [17]. Aptamers have several unique advantages to antibodies such as high stability, ease of modification and low cost, gaining more chances to develop the surface plasmon resonance, electrochemical, fluorometric or colorimetric sensing in recent years [18–21]. The aptamers of CEA and CA125 have been widely used for biosensors by various groups with high specificity and binding affinity [22–25]. Furthermore, comparing with biosensors of single aptamer, the sandwich-type aptasensors by dual aptamers are proved to obtain better sensitivity and reliability because the secondary aptamer can be used to generate and amplify signal [23,26].

In order to offer accurate and faster analyses of cancer related proteins in a clinical laboratory, magnetic beads (MBs) modified with streptavidin, carboxyl and amino are widely used to capture and separate targets from human serum efficiently [27]. However, various of other proteins coexist with cancer related proteins in serum, which can adsorb to the sensing surfaces mainly by reasons of electrostatic adsorption and hydrophobicity, leading to poor sensitivity and inaccuracy in the assay [28]. Polyethylene glycol, amphoteric ionic polymer and hyaluronic acid peptides have been used as antifouling materials to reduce nonspecific adsorption and further improve accuracy of test [29–31]. Due to the various advantages of biocompatibility, facile synthesis and tunable structure, peptides were used as alternative antifouling materials in recent years [32,33]. A peptide (EKEKEKE-PPPPC) was designed by Jiang’s group, including of antifouling and anchoring sequence, demonstrating the excellent feature to resist adsorption of proteins [34].

Herein, we develop a MBs/peptides/CDs-based platform for simultaneous detection of ovarian cancer related proteins, CEA and CA125, as displayed in Scheme 1. Firstly, blue-colored carbon dots (b-CDs) and yellow-colored carbon dots (y-CDs) at a single excitation state are prepared by adding citric acid and thiourea into o-phenylenediamine solution respectively. The amino-modified aptamers, CEA-Apt2 and CA125-Apt2 are conjugated with b-CDs and y-CDs by amide reaction. Next, biotinylated CEA-Apt1, CA125-Apt1 and peptides are mixed with a certain proportion and connected to the surfaces of streptavidin-coated MBs. In the presence of CEA and CA125, Apt1 modified MBs and Apt2 tagged with CDs are conjugated by specific recognition to construct the sandwich structures of MB/Apt1-CEA-Apt2/CD and MB/Apt1-CA125-Apt2/CD in the serum. After magnetic separating and washing, the co-conjugates on the surfaces of MBs are re-dispersed and aptamers are digested by DNase I. Finally, the supernatant, containing two kinds of CDs after magnetic separation, is excited at 375 nm and the signals emitted at 430 nm (b-CDs) and 550 nm (y-CDs) are measured to quantify CEA and CA125, which is of great importance to reduce the background noise and improve sensitivities by using a single excitation wavelength in place of multiple excitations. Meanwhile, peptides (EK) of hydrophilia and electroneutrality on the surfaces of MBs make the sandwich structure of MB/Apt1-protein-Apt2/CD resist non-specific absorption of coexisting proteins better in the complex samples. More importantly, serums from ovarian cancer patients and normal can be simultaneously discriminated and detected, offering the promising opportunities to propose effective, sensitive and simple assay for multiple tumor related protein in clinical diagnosis.

Two colored fluorescence CDs at a single excitation were prepared by hydrothermal treatment. In a procedure of b-CDs, 0.30 g of citric acid, 0.20 g of o-phenylenediamine were dissolved into 60 mL of water, heating for 6 h at 150 °C in the poly-tetrafluoroethylene autoclave. When cooled naturally, the CDs solution was filtered with a 0.22 µm microporous membrane and dialyzed for 48 h in a dialysis bag (1000 Da) with water. Then b-CDs powder was obtained by lyophilizing at − 80 °C, and dissolved into water to 0.2 mg mL−1 by ultrasonic vibration. O-phenylenediamine, thiourea, and glycine were used to synthesize y-CDs. Firstly, 0.20 g of o-phenylenediamine, 0.20 g of thiourea and 0.05 g of glycine were added into water. Next, the solution in lined autoclaves was heated at 170 °C for 12 h. When naturally cooled, it was also filtered, purified and lyophilized. Finally, y-CDs powder was re-dissolved into water to 0.3 mg mL−1 for use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of CEA-Apt2/b-CD and CA125-Apt2/y-CD probes
ce:section.ce:para: The probes were prepared by chemical reaction between amino groups of DNA and carboxyls of CDs. For the conjugates of CEA-Apt2/b-CD, 4.5 mg of N-hydroxysuccinimide and 4 mg of 1-(3-dimethylaminopropyl)− 3-ethylcarbondiimide hydrochloride were added into 950 μL of 0.2 mg mL−1 b-CDs, continuously stirring for 1 h at 25 °C. Next, 50 μL of 10 μM amino-modified CEA-Apt2 was added and stirred at 25 °C for 4 h, keeping at 4 °C overnight. After removing the unbound DNA at a speed of 12000 rpm by ultrafiltration (Millipore, 10 kDa) for 20 min, conjugates were obtained and diluted with 1.0 mL of PBS (pH 7.4, 10 mM). Then the probe was kept at 4 °C for the following assay. For CA125-Apt2/y-CD probe, 950 μL of 0.3 mg mL−1 y-CDs was used and its conjugation was completed as the same way as CEA-Apt2/b-CD.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Simultaneous detection of CEA and CA125
ce:section.ce:para: Firstly, 10 μL of 4 mg mL−1 streptavidin-coated MBs, 60 μL of 1 μM CEA-Apt1, 60 μL of 1 μM CA125-Apt1, 20 μL of 2 μM peptides were mixed evenly in 100 μL of PBS for 30 min at 25 °C, the mixture was repeatedly separated by a magnetic rack and washed with PBS, then aptamers on the surfaces of MBs were re-dispersed with 100 μL of PBS. Secondly, after adding CA125 and CEA, the mixture was incubated at 37 °C for 1 h, repeatedly separated and washed, then co-conjugates of CEA-Apt1 and CA125-Apt1 on the surfaces of MBs were re-dispersed with 100 μL PBS. Thirdly, after adding 100 μL of CEA-Apt2/b-CD and 100 μL of CA125-Apt2/y-CD probes, the mixed solution was incubated at 37 °C for another 1 h, repeatedly separated and washed, then the sandwiched co-conjugates of Apt1-CEA-Apt2/b-CD and Apt1-CA125-Apt2/y-CD on the surfaces of MBs were re-dispersed again with 100 μL of PBS. Finally, after adding 60 μL of 10 U mL−1 DNase I, the mixture was left for another 1.5 h at 37 °C, repeatedly separated and washed, the supernatant containing b-CDs and y-CDs was collected and diluted to a total volume of 600 μL for fluorescence assay.

The morphology of b-CDs and y-CDs and their diameters were characterized by TEM and dynamic light scattering (DLS). CDs are spherical, well-dispersed with an average diameter of about 2.5 ± 0.7 nm for b-CDs ( Figs. 1a) and 2.9 ± 0.8 nm for y-CDs (Fig. 1b). The lattice fringes of b-CDs (Fig. 1c) and y-CDs (Fig. 1d) are well-resolved by high resolution TEM (HRTEM) with a spacing of 0.21 nm, which can be attributed to the (1 0 0) diffraction facets of graphitic carbon [35]. DLS results in Fig. S1 show the average hydrodynamic diameter of b-CDs and y-CDs is 3.1 nm (Fig. S1a) and 3.7 nm (Fig. S1b) respectively, which is a little larger than that observed by TEM for the formation of the hydration layer around the CDs edges.

XPS measurements were tested to reveal the element composition of CDs. As shown in Fig. 1e, three prominent peaks of b-CDs at 531.62, 400.54 and 284.8 eV in the full XPS spectrum are related to O 1 s, N 1 s and C 1 s respectively, indicating that b-CDs mainly consist of Oxygen (O, 17.8 %), Nitrogen (N, 20.54 %), and Carbon (C, 61.66 %). There are four prominent peaks at 531.37, 399.52, 284.8, 162.57 eV in Fig. 1f, corresponding to O 1 s, N 1 s, C 1 s, and S 2p in the XPS spectrum of y-CDs respectively, indicating that y-CDs mainly consist of Oxygen (O, 19.29 %), Nitrogen (N, 13.09 %), Carbon (C, 58.65 %) and Sulsur (S, 8.97 %). XPS spectra were also used for investigating chemical groups of CDs (Fig. S3 and S4), and the results were consistent with analysis of FTIR spectra, indicating the successful preparation of the CDs as expected. The FTIR spectra (Fig. S5) of b-CDs (black curve) and y-CDs (red curve) show that there are various oxygen and nitrogen-containing functional groups on their surfaces, such as hydroxyl, amino, carboxyl groups [35,36]. It is noteworthy that bending vibration of the C-S bond shows a weak peak at 608 cm−1 (red curve), confirming the doping of sulfur element for y-CDs [37,38].

The UV/Vis absorption spectra were recorded for CDs and aptamers tagged with CDs, as shown in Fig. S6. From Fig. S6a, b-CDs exhibit three characteristic absorption peaks at 245, 280 and 360 nm (red curve), which are consistent with n-π * transitions (280 and 360 nm) at CN and CO bonds and π-π * transitions (245 nm) at CC bond. Y-CDs show two absorption peaks at 290 and 420 nm (Fig. S6b, red curve), which are related to n-π * transitions at CN and CO bonds [39]. After Apt2 is linked to the surface of CD, absorption peak at 260 nm is observed (Fig. S6a and S6b, blue curves), indicating the successful conjugation of CEA-Apt2 and b-CDs, CA125-Apt2 and y-CDs. Zeta potentials of CDs-aptamers are all more negative than CDs (Fig. S7), further confirming that CDs surfaces are conjugated with aptamers because DNA molecules are negatively charged.

The fluorescence spectra of b-CDs and y-CDs exhibit the properties of independence on the different excitation wavelengths ( Fig. 2). The emission peak of b-CDs is obtained at 430 nm with excitation from 360 to 410 nm (Fig. 2a), while the emission peak of y-CDs is achieved at 550 nm with excitation from 360 to 460 nm (Fig. 2b). Their florescence independence of excitation wavelength should be related to the fewer surface defects, uniform size and functional groups of CDs [40,41], which is consistent with TEM, XPS and FTIR results of b-CDs and y-CDs. Furthermore, the mixed solution of b-CDs and y-CDs can display the fluorescence peaks at 430 and 550 nm respectively by exciting at any wavelength from 370 to 400 nm (Fig. 2c), without interference, demonstrating two targets can be simultaneously distinguished and detected under a single excitation. Hence, the better emission intensity for b-CDs (430 nm) and y-CDs (550 nm) was obtained at excitation wavelength of 375 nm, 375 nm was chosen for the single excitation wavelength in the following assay.

Surface hydrophilicity of MBs was measured through static water contact angle assessment. The contact angles of streptavidin-coated MBs and streptavidin-coated MBs modified with peptides are 32.05° and 20.84° ( Figs. 3a and 3b), respectively, indicating the peptide-modified MBs possess better hydrophilicity, which contribute to the antifouling property.

The antifouling performance of MBs/peptides was also investigated by adsorption of FITC-BSA. 4 mg mL−1 MBs and 4 mg mL−1 MBs/peptides (measured by MBs concentration) were immersed in 1 mg mL−1 FITC-BSA solution for 0, 8, 16 and 32 h respectively, the supernatant of magnetic separation was obtained for fluorescence detection. MBs adsorb FITC-BSA more easily than MBs/peptides, resulting in less amount of FITC-BSA in the supernatant and the intensity of MBs supernatant decreases significantly (Fig. 3c), while the intensity of MBs/peptides supernatant reduces slowly (Fig. 3d). As the antifouling mechanism mentioned, the hydrophilic or super-hydrophilic surfaces should be constructed for antifouling performance [32,33]. The hydrophilicity is able to reduce nonpolar interactions of functional groups and surfaces，and the electrostatic interactions between surfaces and the charged foulant can be reduced by antifouling materials of electrical neutrality [42]. Thus, the EK sequences of hydrophilicity and electrical neutrality, which are constituted by alternating amino acid residues of negatively charged glutamic acid (E) and positively charged lysine (K), form a strong hydration layer, exhibiting the good resistance to protein adsorption. Hence, MBs/peptides show better antifouling performance than MBs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Simultaneous detection of CEA and CA125 with antifouling peptide
ce:section.ce:para:
Under the optimal conditions (Fig. S8 and S9), the MBs/CDs platform was firstly used to detect CEA and CA125 at a single excitation of 375 nm without peptides adding. As shown in Fig. S10a, fluorescence signals at 430 nm enhanced linearly with concentration of CEA increasing, which indicated that Apt1-CEA-Apt2 sandwiched co-conjugates gathered on the surfaces of MBs, aptamers were digested successfully by DNase I, leading to the release of b-CDs and the enhanced fluorescence intensity for CEA assay. Similarly, fluorescence intensity at 550 nm increased linearly with CA125 adding (Fig. S10b). And CEA and CA125 were simultaneously added, the fluorescence intensities at 430 (b-CDs) and 550 nm (y-CDs) increased (Fig. S10c) at the same time, the current change (F-F0) at 430 nm ΔF
430 nm was linearly related to the concentration of CEA in the range of 1.0–20 ng mL−1, and ΔF
550 nm was correlated with the concentration of CA125 in the range of 5.0–160 U mL−1 (Fig. S10d and S10e), while the limits of detection were 0.23 ng mL−1 and 1.69 U mL−1 (3σ/k) for CEA and CA125, respectively. Then peptides were introduced into the assay, the fluorescence intensities of MBs/peptides/CDs platform at 430 and 550 nm increased simultaneously with CEA and CA125 adding as well ( Fig. 4a), and comparing with the MBs/CDs platform alone, the antifouling MBs/peptides/CDs method made the fluorescence signals enhance obviously and further improved its sensitivity. ΔF
430 nm was examined to be the excellent linear correlation with the concentration of CEA in the range of 0.5–22 ng mL−1 (Fig. 4b) with the detection limit of 0.11 ng mL−1 (3σ/k), and ΔF
550 nm was linear with the concentration of CA125 in the range of 2–175 U mL−1 (Fig. 4c) with the detection limit of 0.57 U mL−1 (3σ/k). Compared with previous reported methods of combining assay for CEA and CA125 (Table S1), the MBs/peptides/CDs platform exhibited the lower detection limit, indicating the advantages of using the antifouling peptides for biosensing interface.

Meanwhile, using the assay of CEA as an example, the excellent binding affinity of aptamer was proved by the equilibrium constant K d of the aptamer-protein interaction and the detection limit. The selected aptamer of CEA showed good binding affinity (5 nM) [43]. Basing on the used concentration of aptamer (100 nM), K d (5 nM), and molar mass of CEA (180 kd), an assumed 1:1 binding mode of aptamer-CEA [44] and the formula of assay response [45] was used to calculate the binding rate, the result showed over 95 % of CEA was bond to its aptamer. The linear equation was adjusted by the calculated concentration of bound CEA and the theoretical limit of detection was obtained to be 0.11 ng mL−1, which was consistent with the detection limit in this assay, further identifying the high binding affinity of the selected aptamer.

An elevated tumor marker with equal to or greater than 2-fold the upper limit of healthy human was usually regarded as indicator of measurable or possible tumor to makes sense to the clinical treatment [46,47]. Hence, in order to reveal the specificity of MBs/peptides/CDs platform for clinical application, Immunoglobulins (IgM and IgG), total protein (TP)and four kinds of tumor related proteins with three times the upper limit of normal concentration, including alpha-fetoprotein (AFP), cancer antigen153 (CA153), cancer antigen199 (CA199), CA125, CEA and neuron specific enolase (NSE), were detected by this method. As shown in Fig. 4d, comparing with the strong fluorescence signals by 10 ng mL−1 CEA and 110 U mL−1 CA125, 50 mg mL−1 TP, 0.2 mg mL−1IgM, 2 mg mL−1 IgG, 60 ng mL−1 AFP, 75 U mL−1 CA153, 90 U mL−1 CA199 and 50 ng mL−1 NSE merely made the smaller fluorescence intensity changes, confirming excellent specificity of the MBs/peptides/CDs platform to avoid the false positive judgment of ovarian cancer. And comparing with MBs/CDs platform (Fig. 4d, black bar), the coexisting proteins made the weaker interfering signals in the MBs/peptides/CDs strategy (Fig. 4d, red bar), which clearly proved the antifouling effect of peptides and better specificity toward target CEA and CA125.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Assay of samples in human serum
ce:section.ce:para: The standard addition method was applied to the normal serum samples and a recovery experiment was conducted. When 4，8，12 ng mL−1 CEA and 20，40，75 U mL−1 CA125 were added into the serum of normal persons respectively. The average recovery of CEA and CA125 was in the range of 91.8–112.8 % and the RSD value was in the range of 1.3–6.4 % as shown in Table 1. The results indicate this method is effective in real serum samples.

Used the cut-off values of CEA (3.3 ng mL−1) and CA125(35 U mL−1) as the references [48,49]. 20 blood samples of tumor patients and 5 normal samples were collected in clinical laboratory and tested by both the clinical ECL immunoassay and this method. Taking the data of this method as the Y-axis, the data of ECL immunoassay as the X-axis, the scatter plots were drawn and the correlation analysis were conducted to prove the reliability of simultaneous detection. As shown in Fig. 4e, the linear correlation analysis of CEA detection between the MBs/peptides/CDs antifouling platform and ECL immunoassay exhibited the correlation coefficient (r), slope and intercept of 0.955, 0.901 and 0.13 ng mL−1 respectively. And a linear correlation analysis of CA125 assay between the antifouling platform and ECL immunoassay (Fig. 4f) demonstrated the correlation coefficient, slope and intercept of 0.976, 0.903 and 0.99 U mL−1 respectively. Hence, according to the significance test of medical statistics for linear correlation (P < 0.05), the correlation coefficients (r > 0.9) between two assays clearly indicated result of this method was significantly correlated with the data from clinical ECL assay for CEA and CA125, which suggested the antifouling platform had a good prospect for clinical test. Meanwhile, the linear correlational analysis of MBs/CDs platform and ECL immunoassay for CEA and CA125 was also finished (Fig. S11), the MBs/peptides/CDs antifouling platform showed the better performance than the MBs/CDs alone.

In brief, a novel MBs/peptides/CDs antifouling strategy of simultaneous detection for dual tumor related proteins in real samples was proposed. Multi-functions of MBs/peptides with better hydrophilicity, lower absorption of non-specific proteins, easy separation and target specific recognition were constructed in the assay. The two contrasting colored fluorescence signals of CDs could be simultaneously observed at a single excitation, without spectra overlap. The simultaneous detection of CEA and CA125 efficiently accomplished, achieving the better sensitivity and specificity, and the results were linearly correlated with clinical assay, providing an alternative way for clinical diagnosis of ovarian cancer. Furthermore, the strategy may greatly help to develop dual/multiple tumor marker detections, which could more effectively improve accuracy of early cancer screening and avoid false positive reactivity in the clinical laboratory.

Mingjing Zou: Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation, Data Curation， Formal analysis， Writing – original draft. Yan Gong: Investigation, Writing – review & editing. Xinxin Sun: Resources, Writing – review & editing. Caifeng Ding: Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation, Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (22074074), the Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province (ZR2020MB065), and the Medical Health Science and Technology Development Program of Shandong Province, China (202111001011).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.snb.2022.132330.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
Supplementary material
.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: G. Funston, W. Hamilton, G. Abel, E.J. Crosbie, F.M. Walter, The diagnostic performance of CA125 for the detection of ovarian and non-ovarian cancer in primary care: A population-based cohort study, PLoS Med. 17 (2020) e1003295.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Mingjing Zou is currently a PhD candidate majoring in Chemistry in Qingdao University of Science and Technology. She is currently working toward biochemical analysis and nano-biosensing.

Yan Gong is currently a PhD candidate majoring in Chemistry in Qingdao University of Science and Technology. Her scientific research interests focus on biochemical analysis based on functional materials.
Xinxin Sun is currently a postgraduate student majoring in Analytical Chemistry in Qingdao University of Science and Technology, her scientific research interests focus on biochemical analysis.

Caifeng Ding is currently a professor in Qingdao University of Science and Technology, China. She received her PhD in the biological functional materials from Nanjing Tech University in 2006. She is the chief director of Key Laboratory of Optic-electric Sensing and Analytical Chemistry for Life Science (Ministry of Education), Head of Innovation Team of Biochemical Analysis (Ministry of Education), and Member of Pharmaceutical Analysis Committee of Chinese Pharmaceutical Association. She was awarded the distinguished young investigator grant of Shandong in 2010, and published more than 60 research papers in top-class and core magazines. Her general research interests lie in optic-electric analysis of tumor markers, drug delivery strategy via multifunctional materials and organic fluorescent probes.
